description: Per-MCP protocol quirks and transport-specific interception strategies

stdio_direct.description: stdio transport via direct subprocess stdout/stderr capture using McpProcessProxy

stdio_docker.description: stdio transport via Docker container, use docker logs --follow --timestamps for I/O capture

sse_http.description: SSE/HTTP transport, intercept at proxy level in internal_api /mcp/{name}/* route, NOT process stdout

quirks: Large tool catalog (53 active tools) — tools/list response can be >50KB
Some tools produce streaming partial output (nmap scan progress)
Docker container stdout mixes JSON-RPC with debug prints — filter non-JSON lines
Tool responses may include base64-encoded binary data (screenshots, pcap snippets)
WSTG test results can be very large (full HTML report in response)
Coverage reports use custom JSON format with nested test case arrays
Knowledge graph responses contain graph serialization (nodes + edges)
Docker container — use docker logs --follow for capture
Attack path algorithm output contains beam search tree structures (deeply nested JSON)
MCTS node data includes scoring vectors with floating-point arrays
Responses can include ASCII art diagrams in text content
Processing time for path computation can exceed 60s — adjust timeout
Session management — tool calls maintain stateful sessions across requests
Command output can stream partial results (shell command execution)
Resource status responses include live connection metadata
Large streaming output from long-running commands — be prepared for multi-MB responses
Decompiled code responses can be very large (full function C pseudocode)
Function list for large binaries can contain 10000+ entries
Xref graph format uses custom edge/node JSON serialization
String extraction returns raw binary strings — may contain non-UTF8 data
Generally small payloads — search results, host details, DNS
Rate limited by Shodan API — tool errors may be rate limit responses
API key required — watch for key exposure in debug logs
SSE transport — intercept at HTTP proxy level, NOT process stdout
Scan results use Burp-specific finding schema with severity levels
May produce large scan result sets for comprehensive scans
Connection to Burp Suite API requires separate authentication
Vulnerability results follow Nessus plugin output format
Scan status responses include progress percentage — not standard MCP notifications
Report format can be very large (full PDF-equivalent JSON)
Requires Nessus API connection — watch for credential exposure
DESTRUCTIVE tools: block_ip, isolate_host, kill_process — require enhanced logging
Alert format follows Wazuh JSON alert schema (nested rule/decoder/agent)
Agent status includes real-time connection state
Destructive action confirmation may use non-standard response format
DESTRUCTIVE tools: firewall_iptables, firewall_nftables — require sudo
Needs privilege escalation — log sudo usage events
Hardening results include compliance check scores
System status responses contain real-time system metrics
Hypothesis format uses structured threat hunting methodology JSON
Splunk query results can be very large (multiple MB of log entries)
HEARTH database format uses custom schema for hunting artifacts
Hunt reports combine multiple data sources
POTENTIALLY VERY LARGE output — memory dump analysis can produce multi-MB responses
Process list format includes virtual/physical memory addresses
Network connection format maps to Volatility plugin output schema
Registry and malfind results can be binary-heavy — may include hex dumps
Output preview truncation CRITICAL for this MCP
Analysis report format combines multiple analysis modules
YARA match results include matched rule details and offset data
TI enrichment responses aggregate from multiple threat intel sources
Sandbox results can include behavioral analysis traces (large)
Event format follows MISP JSON event schema (complex nested structure)
Attribute format includes MISP-specific type taxonomy
Correlation results can span multiple events and organizations
Feed sync operations can transfer large datasets
SSE transport via HTTP — not stdio, intercept at proxy level
Deep crawl responses can be very large (multiple pages of content)
Structured extraction responses depend on user-provided JSON schema
Server must be running before MCP calls — not auto-started
